title: 2025/12/11 EDSI
displayTitle: 2025-12-10 - Economic Development, Science and Innovation Committee - Briefing on the upcoming reports of the Science System Advisory Group
committee: Economic Development, Science and Innovation Committee
topic: Briefing on the upcoming reports of the Science System Advisory Group
description: Briefing on the upcoming reports of the Science System Advisory Group Science System Advisory Group Sir Peter Gluckman, Chair Hema Sridhar, Project lead and secretariat of the SSAG
descriptionSummary: Briefing on the upcoming reports of the Science System Advisory Group. Science System Advisory Group. Sir Peter Gluckman, Chair

chunks.text: Is there some special reason you know about Vanessa? No, no. I was just- She asks, mm. Oh. Yeah, yeah. Um... (bell rings) (bell rings again) There it is. A. Yes. Good morning, Sir Peter. Good morning, Paramjit. How are you? Ready for half an hour?

I'm always re- I'm always ready to talk about science, you know that. Yes, I know that. (laughs) Thank you so much. And is, uh, Hema joining you as well? She may. She'll be at home, so I presume she'll join in from the s- uh, in a minute. So- Okay.... uh- So you think there has to be- ... we're doing it both from home. So you want us to wait for her or? No, no. Please go- proceed.

Uh- Your time is important. All right. So, th- we want to focus on the second report. Under- Understand. And yeah, so if you could, um, open with some remarks, like a very, very quick summary of the second report- Yes. then we will go on to questions from members. Thank you. Right. Well, I think the second report really has a lot of detail in it about what is necessary for science and innovation to really contribute to New Zealand's economic, social, environmental wellbeing. It's far more than just about structural change,

which was largely dealt with in the first report. And I think the key issue is, there's a lot of, uh, what I'd call nuanced detail that's in the second report, which is necessary to be understood. I think the most important thing is to recognize that the system is fragile, because it's been chronically underfunded for at least 40 years. And that without, while structural change was essential, and the report talks a lot about the details of structural change, is the fundamentals that New Zealand failed, unlike other small economies, to replace manufacturing in

the ni- when globalization occurred, with understanding that the future was s- innovation and technology based. More specifically, the report talks about the needs to fix the space between i- intellectual property development and IPO, more detail on issues such as need for research foun- or res- a national research foundation, which has been announced, issues around intellectual property, and a lot about the fundamentals of getting innovation from the science system, but equally focusing on the importance of stewardship report, stewardship science,

issues such as science diplomacy, international relations, and so forth. I'll stop there in the interest of time. Thank you so much. Uh, so we have, uh, a number of questions here. Uh, we'll start with Scott Willis. Uh, thank you, Sir Peter. Um, and I- I'm really interested in your comments, because withdrawal of support for social sciences and humanities is something your reports didn't consider, but is now a government decision, and that sort of intersects your recommendations. And, and given the, um, challenges that we face in the 21st century, and our

uniqueness as an island nation, we've got climate change and all of those natural hazards, how unusual and problematic is the removal of the support for these social sciences and humanities? And do- what are- Well, first of all, it's, I'm not sure that's a fair statement. A lot of science, social science and humanities, is supported through other mechanisms. But it's also true to say that across the Western world, governments have been looking at the science system in a much more utilitarian a- way. And, and I think that that's an unfortunate global

trend. And I think we, in our report, and if you go back also to our first report, and to our universities report in particular, we focus on the importance of universities as the intellectual wellspring of New Zealand, the importance of social science, which is funded in many ways across government, the importance of humanities, which is sadly only fundish- largely funded through the PBRF.

But I think if one looks, I, I, oh, I can't talk yet to announcements from the Prime Minister's Science Innovation Advisory Council, which I'm a member, but there is a strong consideration that social science, humanities remain part of the science portfolio.

Right. Thank you. Uh, supplementary? Supplementary. Yes. Um, so in relation to, to that, that funding, the UK and the EU have recently justified massive multi-year investments in research by saying that every, every pound or euro pays back eight to 11 times that amount to society. And estimates for the US are, are, are much higher. Are we losing, or what are we losing for every dollar that we, that we withdraw or, or don't invest in research?

Look, I think-Mr. Willis, your question is the right question. I think it's unfortunately been a bipartisan issue since 1970. If you look at the graph in our, uh, the first graph in our report, you'll see we've been diverging in terms of productivity ever since then from everybody we would compare ourselves to. Nobel Prizes were given both by, t- too many years ago, and even la- this year, t- to, to economists who pointed out the importance of science and innovation to economies.

And it's particularly important in small economies that lost their way economically with globalization because of the loss of manufacturing, and that the next stage in economic growth is clearly being technology and innovation, and that should have been clear to both sides of the house for 30 or 40 years. And the numbers from our first report, and the detail in the second report, show that New Zealand continues to think it can put it off for another day.

Now, yes, the system needed fixing up. And there's no debate that there were issues in the system, and the, and the government is making decisions which I think are sensible in that way. But at the end of the day, New Zealand will not get the benefit. If you invest one dollar, you can only get a small return. If you invest $10, you get a bigger return.

And if you look around the world, New Zealand is the exception amongst small economies, and in fact, I had a discussion with one of the Nobel laureates who got the prize the other da- uh, this year in economics, and he said New Zealand's challenge is gonna be, does it understand that innovation and science are key to being a First World country?

Or is New Zealand making a choice to go the other way? (laughs) Now, I'm paraphrasing what he said- (laughs)... but that's the reality. Um, I'm primary from Dr. Hamish Campbell. Excellent. Th- thank, thank you, Sir Peter. Um, and I to- totally agree and echo your words about science, uh, science and innovation. Um, my question is, um, i- it's all well and good setting up, uh, a central funding agency, uh, but the devil is always going to be in the detail.

And you mention some of, almost kind of, kind of conflicting things in the report. How do we ensure that we actually have a wide range of research funded? Uh, y- and you mention in the report, um, a country of only five million people cannot do everything, so priority setting is key to the research sys- system. However, I would actually say that we're actually very poor at knowing where research and innovation is going to go.

So, how do we make sure that we're actually doing the best science that's actually gonna lead to the best outcomes rather than actually hav- having, in effect, people trying to pick winners? Well, what you don't want to do is pick losers, so you've got to be careful. (laughs) (laughs) Uh, uh, I think there's a lot of evidence in science policy that says the kinds of tests we put into the report, and which I believe are gonna be re- endorsed by the Prime Minister's Science Innovation Council, are important. We've gotta...

first of all, it's gotta be quality. Bad research is a waste of money. Secondly, it's got to be, um, uh, be, uh, something that can justify the taxpayers' expenditure. And thirdly, it's gotta justify why we do it in New Zealand. 'Cause as you said, not even UK can do everything in science, why c- New Zealand cannot at 5 million.

And our report talks to that. But you do need the full gamut of research, from, from discovery research, and you need mechanisms that allow for the next Rocket Lab or the next, uh, great innovation that comes out of the universities to flourish.

And I think that you'll see that both the work that follows from our report and, I think, in the, w- will allow for that. But at the end of the day, you're quite right. The mechanisms that the New Zealand Research Foundation uses need to be i- ne- need to be very robust and very, very best practice internationally.

And there's a lot going on, wearing my hat as President of the National Science Council, I can tell you that just picking up what we, how we do it now and amalgamating it will not work. We need to have a much m- and so I've been advocating we need international expertise engaged in the New Zealand Research Foundation.

Mm-hmm. Sir Hamish? And, and kind of following on with the international, uh, kind of theme, um, there's a, it mentions in your report about looking at outputs and probably moving away from, in effect, publications. However, we are, uh, the scientific system is very international and we want our scientists to be internationally competitive, um, a- and we also want to attract international scientists here to, here to New Zealand.

How can New Zealand move away from maybe publications yet still retain- I think- ... their international competitiveness? Well, number one is, I think if you look at the global science community, of which I have a major role in, everybody, and particularly Europe, is trying to find alternate ways of measuring science performance than bibliometrics, which is a very self-serving system, as you know, uh, Dr. Campbell.

Huge. (laughs) The problem is, uh, that you want short-term measures of quality and you want long-term measures of impact, 'cause impact comes later. And yes, there are valuable things in bibliometrics, but from the point of view of the New Zealand citizen, we need something more than that. And the science community internationally, and particularly the European Commission, as well, and the various groups around the U- EU and the International Science Council, are all trying to find mechanisms to get a more complete way of

looking at science than that. As you know, Dr. Campbell, the-... just, just 'cause it's published in Nature doesn't mean it's great science. (laughs) And, and so less than half the papers in Nature actually stand the test of time. Hmm. Mm-hmm. So we have to, we have to look at them in a sli- in a different way, but we haven't found a better way.

It's a bit like with old saying about democracy, that's, uh, uh, it's a- Mm-hmm.... bad form of government, but nobody's thought anything better. Yeah. Hmm. Um, primary from Dr. David Wilson. Good morning, Sir Peter. Um, very welcome the report, very much welcome the report. I'm gonna challenge you a little bit.

Um, uh, yeah, because I'm really interested in the innovation part of this whole new ecosystem we've set up. And, and I agree with the, uh, attempt, really, to integrate and provide a systems approach for our national innovation system. Um, where I'm going to challenge is from the bottom up. Um, internationally, when you're looking at innovation, you're looking to do things at a regional level in a lot of cases, and the connection between the national innovation system and what happens on the ground, I, A, uh, i.e.

the science push versus the market pull, how do we pull those things together? Because the old publish and perish is not working. And, you know, when you're talking to clusters of, uh, innovators and businesses in a, at a regional level, they'll be looking far more at what their local conditions are, what their market challenges are for their own particular sector, and how can they pull in the science, and the innovation, and interact with our national innovation system?

Often, it's just a repeat of the ivory towers missing the point. Well, I don't ... Well, uh, number one is, I agree with everything you've said. Cities are often the major units of innovation around the world. There's no debate about that, for a whole lot of reasons, agglomeration effects and, and working with work.

Secondly, our first report and our universities report talk a lot about the boundaries between, uh, the academic side of science and the applied science. But the biggest thing, and we spent a lot of time in this report, probably a disproportionate amount, and you might ask why, on that interface between the, between, if you like, discovery and application, and in particular what we call the IP to IPO phase. New Zealand doesn't have it right.

We failed with Callaghan Innovation for a whole lot of reasons we don't need, need to go into now. We want to follow the best practices from Israel, Ireland, Singapore and so forth in that areas, Finland and so forth. It's buried in, in the detail of understanding that the interface is not tr- is not a sharp transition. It's a matter of sharing risk, and how that risk is shared over time from being 100%, if you like, state risk, to 100% private risk.

And we spent a lot of time in the report trying to take, if you, the, the policy community through understanding that. And the solution that we've looked at, which certainly overseas people think if we can adopt it will be best in class, is to link it very much to a s- to the New Zealand Institute of Advanced Technology Agency, and provide the risk-sharing mechanisms through that.

And that, with a hub and spoke model, should help. But you're right at the end. Until the institutions of science and the institutions of private sector get closer together, it's a psychological thing as much as a practical thing. And the issue is when you only invest 0.6% of GDP, at best, from the private sector, that's well below the international evidence you need 0.8 to 0.9% to start to get the infrastructure in the private sector, and the interest of those who can invest at scale into the system.

70% of the research in the world is done by no- is supported by the multinational corporations, and we don't have enough activity here to justify them investing in here and creating the complete ecosystem. You only have to look around the world. Every other small advanced economy has done it.

The question is, will we remain one? Mm-hmm. Yeah, just a- Yeah.... quick supplementary. Yes, Sir Peter, I agree with everything (laughs) you just said as well. And, um, some thought I think perhaps needs to go into how we make those connections on the ground, 'cause every ... You know, even if you took earth sciences, every single region in New Zealand would have a different idea about what the use of that research would, could be, or would be in their region. You know, each, each region has its own distinct regional economy.

So those connections to draw in the private sector, where they can see value that's going to be added to their endeavor, I think that's gonna be critical. I agree. But then you do need a system that, as I said, of risk-sharing in the appropriate way. And that's what- Yeah.... we, that's what we've been recommending in our report, and that's why we spent so much time on it. Uh, because whether it's risk-sharing for something advanced technology in Auckland, or something around an agricultural technology in Hawke's Bay, it's

the same phenomenon. Risk-sharing occurs between the public and private se- It's a gradual transition, not a sharp transition. Thank you, Sir Peter. Um, new primary from Reuben Davidson. Thank you, Madam Chair, and, and good morning, Sir Peter. Thank you for your, um, excellent answers so far. Um, I keep hearing a lot of concern that New Zealand's best scientists are heading overseas. Should these reforms have s- have stopped that?

And if not, how can we, or what will? Well, they're heading over ... Well, number one is we don't have a lot of detail, but I think your suspicion and my suspicion is that we don't have, we don't have a stable system. Uh, secondly, uh, uh, and we are losing scientists of merit who we've spent a lot of money investing in training.

Uh, if you look at it through in that way, we're exporting something more than just-... knowledge, we're exporting investment. Retaining them is a matter of what we've talked about already in this discussion, Ruben, namely, uh, uh, we've got to have a healthy ecosystem, a vibrant innovation system, bilateral support from all the political parties that science and innovation are a core part of growth, whether it's for stewardship purposes or economic growth, both require, uh, us to have a robust, healthy system.

And unfortunately, and I, and I'll be blunt, I think every past administration has always said this is a luxury that can be put for, off till another time. A- And whether it's one side of the House or the other, that's been the case for 50 years. And I think N- it, New Zealand's exceptionalism is now paying a price in a world that's now rapidly using technology, in- and innovation, not just in economic growth, but in social development and policy development and so forth, environmental management. We've got a lot to catch up.

Yeah. Uh, new question from Scott Willis. Yeah. Th- thank you again, Sir, Sir Gluckman. Your report suggested that between 0.5 and 0.7%, or as you've just mentioned, 0.8 to 0.9% of GDP going into the foundations of public research was a critical threshold for the effectiveness of the entire system, and I think you've described some of the consequences of the lack of funding over subsequent governments.

But, you know, how far are we from this critical threshold right now? And, and what do we need to do? Well, funnily enough, it's not a lot of money (laughs) I mean, but, uh, I mean, it's significant money and every dollar of the taxpayers' money really counts. But in the overall expenditure of government, we're talking about another 0.1, 0.2, 0.3% of GDP. It would, couldn't all be absorbed in one year. I mean, I think there, it needs to be built up. It nearly happened in the 2010, '11 period until the earthquake in Christchurch shifted,

uh, uh, the government's priorities for obvious acute reasons. But there had been a- an agreement to do so at that time. Uh, and I think, I would hope, as Finland has done, the Finnish Government has achieved, which is a bipartisan agreement on the investment strategy for R&D for Finland for the next decade. And there has been ...

they've achieved it across the House. And I would like to make a plea to all of you. Science and innovation is not a partisan issue. Mm-hmm. Yes, we live in a healthy democracy where you should debate and you do debate many things. But I think whether we want science innovation for economic growth, grow our private sector, to make better decisions as a country, to be a place which promotes the intellectual wellspring of New Zealand, all said and done, we are an innovative country, psychologically at least, or whether it's just to look after the

basic assets of New Zealand. New Zealand is ... Every government's primary responsibility should be stewardship of our people, our culture, our economy, our infrastructure, our environment, and our international relationships. Coming back to the question about keep retaining, uh, attracting talent. All of that needs science innovation at a l- a level we don't currently have.

Yes, we have fixed the s- not fixed, we're in the process of getting a system which is better developed for the 21st century, from what was developed in 1991. But that's, that's the architecture. It doesn't actually change things on the ground. And we are in a global war now for talent. Uh, coming back to an earlier question from one of the other members of the panel, the opportunities that exist because of geopolitical change, for New Zealand- Mm-hmm.

... to be attractive for other reasons- Hmm.... is at a time when other countries are investing heavily, Singapore, European Union, France itself, other European countries, to attract talent. China is being very aggressive with European and American talent as well.

New Zealand's got something special to offer in some areas. Yes, we may not be at the cutting edge of some areas of science, but we certainly are at the cutting edge of some areas, and we've certainly got the potential to do, at scale- Can I supplement?... many things that we are not doing.

Mm-hmm. Uh, supplementary, Scott Willis. Supplementary. So, um, the new body, Research Funding New Zealand, somewhat resembles your proposed Research Council, but there are also differences in, are you concerned about the lack of independence of Research Funding New Zealand from MBIE and, and that we might lose some expertise or, um, that if it's not transferred from the Marsden Funds Council and Health Research Council?

Is that- Look- ... is that a ... Yeah. Uh, uh, Minister Reti and I have had lengthy discussions about this, because there's pros and cons of one extreme or the other. And, you know, having it buried in the ministry as the current Science Board is, in the case of the Endeavor Fund, has problems, and there's been real concerns.

Equally, being totally remote from the taxpayers' interests is also a concern. Whether we've got the, whether the minister's got this right or not will be the devil of the detail of how the board is structured, how the board operates, and the quality of the board. I think that there were concerns obviously about the Science Board being somewhat of a-... I don't know, I better be careful about my words, but... 'Cause they were good people, but their level of independence was not there. At least the NZRF board, uh, should be established as a distinct entity,

albeit that it is not inappropriate, and it's part of the social contract for science for the Crown to have a view on priorities. It's when it goes from a view on priorities to a view on which grants get funded or which scientists get Mm-hmm. Mm-hmm. ... that the problems emerge. And I think that's gonna be ... However this finally settles, that's the key issue. Where does ... You know, so it's entirely appropriate for the government of the day to say, "We want to foster more research in agritech or more research in- in- in- in digital," or whatever.

It's not approp- ... When they say, "Peter Gluckman shouldn't get it and, and Scott Willis should," and it's probably not appropriate to go as far as United States has done, where they've said research on gun control is not, is not- Mm-hmm.... something to be funded by the state. So there is a, a- Mm-hmm.

... subtlety in the social contract, irrespective of the organizational structure. Because the Crown could still, even under the current system, have said, "Marsden Fund cannot fund this," or, "The AHRC cannot fund this." So I think we've got to be careful to separate out the practical side of how, in a small country, the taxpayers' money's properly looked after, from the decision-making over which research is done, which I think international best practice is clear about the importance of extended ...

Of the Haldane Principle. But I think increasingly for some kinds of research, extended peer review is needed, which brings the stakeholder into the consideration. Mm-hmm. New primary- I was gonna s- Oh, sorry. Um, a supplementary, Dr. Hamill. Uh, thank you. And, and, P- Peter, you mentioned about EU investing a lot of money and there is opportunities, um, and also you talk about attracting talent here, but also attracting foreign funding here. What should New Zealand do?

Uh, in Canterbury we've been quite successful with a few EU grants. What should we be doing to actually be attracting more international grants to New Zealand? Well, we've gotta be out there, Hamish. I mean- Mm-hmm.... our scientists need to be exposed to ... I mean, at the end of the day, collaboration is built on relationships, as you know. And therefore, our scientists need to be out there. We have the lowest international science diplomacy profile in, in the Western world. I've suggested in the report ways in which it could be improved for virtually no

money, very ... You know, under a half a million dollars probably in total a year. But it needs to be done, because if you're not building the bridges between scientists and scientists, funders and funders, we're not part ... We're not, uh, heavily engaged, for instance, in the Research on Research initiative.

We're not engaged in a lot of different things around the world that to- ... The EU is a great partnership, and I was glad to be part of the start of those discussions, before it t- was taken further by the later administration. But it's not the be-all and end-all. There's many other aspects of social science with foundations, with other relationships, and the international science system is evolving. It comes back to our brain drain issues, lots of other issues. If we're not out there with disc- uh, presenting New Zealand and having a scientific

dip- diplomatic effort of note, we cannot win. And so again, the report is not suggesting large amounts of money in that space, but it is saying we need to do more. Secondly, I think that where countries invest in international partnerships, they always get a return. The Catalyst Fund is very small.

I would like to see, and I've said to previous ministers, I would like to see the amount of money available for international partnerships far greater and less targeted to ... Too much of our international science relationship is based on past visits of prime ministers or presidents and a specific, "Let's announce this fellowship," or something.

It needs to be based on strategy for New Zealand's interests- Yes, totally.... not based on we have to ... Oh, the minister, Prime Minister X has visited or we've percent- ... And we've therefore announced a fellowship or something. It's gotta be how ... You know, let's say something like, "In applied, uh, artificial intelligence for a given area, which are the leading countries to partnership?" It may not be the, uh, where we have the relationship now.

It might be another country. For example, I'll just throw it up. Our closest geographical neighbor technically is France through New Caledonia. We, France has got some of the most AI-developed relationships in the world. Are we seeing France as an obvious partner in AI?

I'm not sure we are. Mm-hmm. I'm just using it as an example, not as a, uh, recommendation (laughs) . (laughs) All right. Uh, final question, which is a supplementary from Reuben Davidson. Thank you, Madam Chair. Um, and thank you, Sir Peter, for excellent answers this morning. We all have different perspectives, but one thing I think we'd all be interested in is whether you'd be able to share that report, the low-cost one, um-... if that's something that you'd be able to share with the committee.

Uh, it's in here. Uh, I, I can give you more detail, Reuben, and happy to do so, but basically if you read our section on internationalization and social diplo-, we said-... other countries have developed a concept of scientists as ambassadors, where they actually take three or four leading scientists and say, "Your area is either by discipline or by region,"

and they put them out there to build the relationships. To be blunt, if you sent an official, and I'm not criticizing officials from either MFAT or MBIE, they're not gonna get entry to the, eh, you know, "We want to see what y- we can do in, let's say, agritech." They don't get to see the head of the institute or the leading scientists. You send our most distinguished scientists to talk to other countries in agritech, you will find opportunities that haven't been there before.

Uh, just to give you an example from my own background, when I was chief scientist, I developed a relationship with, uh, the president of the European Commission's, uh, chief strategist on the value of the IDI and how the IDI could be used.

Now, various things got in the way in the long run, uh, but in the end, there are opportunities we don't look at and see. Uh, you know, it's, the, it's, I'm not criticizing officials, but they do not have the entry that, let's say, a Sir Peter Hunter or, or, uh, somebody like myself has to actually build and identify the opportunities that are there. So, we suggested that New Zealand should have four, three to four science ambassadors that might be paid a small honorarium, and obviously their costs met, and they were designed to go out three or four times a year

and actually target particular areas to identify opportunities and build relationships. If I knock on the door, I can see, just to take France's example, the head of ANR, their principal funding system, or the head of CNRS easily. If an official from ... The one official we have in Europe from MBIE, one official who might get to Paris two or three times a year, he's not gonna get that entry, or she's not gonna get that entry.

Yeah. That's just the reality of the world. Mm. Mm. Yeah. So, Sir Peter, um, thank you so much for your time this morning. And c- can I also thank you on behalf of our committee for the contribution that you continues to, um, uh, continue to make to our science systems. Thank you so much. Thank you. And thank you for the committee, all of you, for being non-partisan and taking this seriously in an appropriate way. That is how New Zealand will progress, when we can identify these areas which are fundamental to New Zealand's progress.
So, thank you, madam Good message. Thank you. Thank you. Thank you.
